*Content was generated by Bard*

Google Cloud Compute Engine (GCE) is a cloud computing service that offers virtual machines (VMs) on demand. GCE VMs can be used for a variety of workloads, including web serving, data processing, and machine learning.

GPUs (graphics processing units) are specialized processors that are designed to accelerate graphics and video processing. GPUs can be used to improve the performance of GCE VMs that are running graphics-intensive workloads.

To use GPUs with GCE, you can create a VM instance with a GPU attached. You can then install the necessary drivers and software to use the GPU.

Here are some of the benefits of using GPUs with GCE:

* Improved performance for graphics-intensive workloads
* Reduced costs for running graphics-intensive workloads
* Increased flexibility for running graphics-intensive workloads

If you are running graphics-intensive workloads on GCE, then you should consider using GPUs to improve performance and reduce costs.

Here are some of the things you can do with GPUs on GCE:

* Train machine learning models
* Render 3D graphics
* Process video
* Encode and decode video
* Stream games

GPUs are a powerful tool that can be used to improve the performance of a wide variety of workloads. If you are running graphics-intensive workloads on GCE, then you should consider using GPUs to improve performance and reduce costs.

### Deploying ML APIs using GKE

1. Pre-requisites
2. Reserving Static IP
3. Creating Compute Engine instances with GPUs
4. Connecting to the Instance via SSH
5. Configuring NVIDIA driver
6. Starting Docker Image

#### Pre-requisites

- Acccess to GCP with a proper billing account along with Compute Engine and GPU devices
- Service Accounts and Network settings must have been configured properly
- Google Cloud SDK (`gcloud` and `gsutil`) must have been installed and configured
- The following environment variables must have been set

```shell
PROJECT_ID=<GCP Project ID>
REGION=<Region>
ZONE=<Zone>
SERVICE_ACCOUNT=<Service Account>
SUBNET=<Subnet>
```

#### Reserving Static IP

```shell
gcloud compute addresses create h2o-llm-studio-ip --region $REGION
gcloud compute addresses describe h2o-llm-studio-ip --region $REGION
```

- Set IP Address

```shell
IP_ADDRESS=<IP_ADDRESS>
```

#### Creating Compute Engine instances with GPUs

```shell
gcloud compute instances create h2o-llm-studio \
--project=$PROJECT_ID \
--zone=$ZONE \
--machine-type=n1-standard-16 \
--network-interface=address=$IP_ADDRESS,network-tier=PREMIUM,subnet=$SUBNET \
--maintenance-policy=TERMINATE \
--provisioning-model=STANDARD \
--service-account=$SERVICE_ACCOUNT \
--scopes=https://www.googleapis.com/auth/cloud-platform \
--accelerator=count=4,type=nvidia-tesla-p100 \
--tags=allow-10101,allow-8000,http-server \
--create-disk=auto-delete=yes,boot=yes,device-name=h2o-llm-studio-disk,image=projects/ml-images/global/images/c2-deeplearning-pytorch-2-0-gpu-v20230615-debian-11-py310,mode=rw,size=250,type=projects/$PROJECT_ID/zones/$ZONE/diskTypes/pd-balanced \
--no-shielded-secure-boot \
--shielded-vtpm \
--shielded-integrity-monitoring \
--labels=goog-ec-src=vm_add-gcloud \
--reservation-affinity=any \
--metadata=google-logging-enabled=true,startup-script='#!/bin/bash
/opt/deeplearning/install-driver.sh'
```

#### Connecting to the Instance via SSH

- Creating tunnel

```shell
gcloud compute start-iap-tunnel h2o-llm-studio 22 --local-host-port=localhost:6000 --zone $ZONE --project $PROJECT_ID
```

- Connecting SSH

```shell
gcloud compute ssh h2o-llm-studio --project $PROJECT_ID --zone $ZONE --tunnel-through-iap
```

#### Configuring NVIDIA driver

```shell
sudo su -
```

```shell
nvidia-smi
```

```shell
sudo /opt/deeplearning/install-driver.sh
```

```shell
sudo apt-get update \
    && sudo apt-get install -y nvidia-container-toolkit-base
sudo nvidia-ctk runtime configure --runtime=docker
sudo systemctl restart docker
```

#### Starting Docker Image

```shell
mkdir data
mkdir output

docker run \
    -it -d \
    --name h2o-llm-studio \
    --runtime=nvidia \
    --shm-size=64g \
    --init \
    --rm \
    -u `id -u`:`id -g` \
    -p 80:10101 \
    -p 8000:8000 \
    -e H2O_WAVE_ADDRESS=http://0.0.0.0:10101 \
    -e H2O_WAVE_APP_ADDRESS=http://0.0.0.0:8000 \
    -v `pwd`/data:/workspace/data \
    -v `pwd`/output:/workspace/output \
    gcr.io/vorvan/h2oai/h2o-llmstudio:nightly
```

```shell
docker logs h2o-llm-studio
```
